stories.paragraph: In the autumn of 1880, Tommaso and Lucia Ricci stepped off the steamship Canopic at Castle Garden in New York with four children and two wooden trunks. Tommaso, a stonemason from outside Avellino, carried a linen pouch of seed garlic from their village. The harbor was crowded with immigrants, officials, and steam.

The cousin who had promised them work had left for Pittsburgh. The Riccis took two rooms above a livery stable on Elizabeth Street in Little Italy. Lucia stood at a narrow window holding eleven-month-old Salvatore wrapped against a sharp New York wind, while the older children unpacked a trunk on the plank floor.

Tommaso found work on the great bridge rising across the East River, chipping granite for the approaches. He stood on a stone pier in a leather apron, mallet and chisel in hand, shaping a massive granite block while other masons and laborers moved among scaffolds and ropes above the river.

Six-year-old Salvatore sat on the tenement stoop reading a rent notice aloud to his father, translating English into Italian. Nearby he bargained with an Irish delivery boy over the price of a sack of coal. His mother Lucia watched from the doorway, proud and uneasy at his quick tongue.

The older Ricci siblings made their lives in the neighborhood. Giuseppe laid bricks on a scaffold on a tenement wall. Rosa stood at a fruit stall with her husband and small children. Carmela bent over a sewing machine in her seamstress shop on Grand Street, a painted sign above the door.

Salvatore sat on a stone bench in the courtyard of City College, a Dickens novel open on his lap and a paper-wrapped lunch beside him. He wore a worn jacket and stiff white collar. Behind him rose the college's gothic arches and tall windows, and other students passed in twos and threes.

In a high-ceilinged Lower Manhattan counting room in 1902, Salvatore stood at a tall wooden desk in a stiff collar, pointing to a correction in a leather-bound ledger. Across from him stood Eleanor Bradford, a bookkeeper in a dark skirt and blouse, her expression sharp with interest. Gaslight hung overhead.

Salvatore and Eleanor married at New York City Hall in 1906. They stood before a clerk on a marble floor, flanked by only two witnesses: Salvatore's sister Carmela in a dark dress, and Eleanor's cousin Prudence in a plain coat. A folded telegram bearing the single word NO lay on a nearby table.

In the kitchen of a narrow Brooklyn house, Lucia and Eleanor stood side by side at a wooden table rolling pasta dough. A small green notebook labeled Receipts, Ricci lay open beside a pot of simmering fish chowder on the stove. Strings of garlic and dried herbs hung from a beam above them.

In 1908, Eleanor's Beacon Hill mother visited the Brooklyn house. She sat upright on a horsehair settee in a dark traveling dress and small hat, holding a porcelain coffee cup. Eleanor and Lucia sat opposite her, a coffee service and a plate of biscotti on the low table between them, the room formal and quiet.

Salvatore and Eleanor stood on the front steps of their Brooklyn house with their three children. Thomas, a boy of about eight, held a schoolbook. Prudence, a girl of about five, held her mother's hand. Little Annie, about two, clutched a rag doll. Behind them the door stood open onto a narrow hallway.

A split panel of two family scenes. On the left, the three children in summer clothes sail a small wooden boat in Plymouth harbor with their great-aunts, a New England lighthouse on the shore behind them. On the right, the same children at a long Bronx dinner table on Arthur Avenue, making struffoli with their Italian cousins under a hanging lamp.
